topic: Someone receives an apology letter years after the incident
raw_output:
[story 1]

The envelope, thick and cream-colored, sat accusingly on the cluttered desk. Liam picked it up with the tip of his thumbnail, the paper feeling offensively smooth beneath his touch. It was a letter, dated nearly a decade ago, an apology for something small, something he’d long since managed to sweep under the rug. He unfolded the single sheet, his brow furrowing deeper as he scanned the familiar, clumsy script. A slow, almost imperceptible tightening began in his jaw. He let out a sharp, quiet exhale, the air catching in his chest. He folded the paper back up with unnecessary force, the sound a small, brittle snap in the quiet office. He pushed it aside, focusing instead on the stack of overdue invoices, the numbers suddenly seeming far more compelling than any belated sentiment.

[story 2]

Sarah stared at the post in her hands, the official seal seeming absurdly formal against the worn texture of the envelope. She traced the embossed lettering with a fingertip, her knuckles whitening slightly. The memory of the event—the awkwardness, the fallout—flashed behind her eyes, accompanied by a familiar, hot prickle low in her stomach. She reread the carefully worded paragraphs, each sentence landing with a dull thud. A muscle jumped in her cheek. She crumpled the letter once, twice, the paper protesting under her grip. With a decisive shove, she tossed it toward the recycling bin, the movement jerky and impatient. She immediately turned back to her screen, forcing her gaze onto the complex data, anything to distract from the lingering sense of unwelcome obligation.

[story 3]

Mark found the letter tucked between a utility bill and a takeout menu. He didn't bother with the formalities; he just pulled it out, his gaze skimming the page with practiced disinterest. The handwriting was undeniably the other person’s, a familiar, slightly rushed slant to the letters. A low growl escaped his throat, a sound more like gravel shifting than actual speech. He didn't read every word; he absorbed the general tone—the saccharine regret, the excessive emphasis on remorse. He smoothed the paper flat on the table, his fingers drumming an uneven rhythm against the surface. He picked up his coffee mug, the ceramic warm against his palms, and took a deliberate, long sip, his eyes fixed on the swirling brown liquid, refusing to acknowledge the contents of the paper for the foreseeable future.
